findings.title: Error path via onError callback is never tested
findings.description: The production code (evening-pipeline.ts lines 106-110) catches errors in the cron handler and forwards them to the onError callback. This error path is never tested. There is no test that verifies: (1) what happens when reportGenerator.generate() throws, (2) what happens when memoryExtractor.extract() throws, (3) what happens when memoryOrchestrator.persistExtractionResults() throws, or (4) that the onError callback receives the correct wrapped Error with cause. The cron handler silently swallows errors via the default no-op handleError, meaning a broken pipeline would go unnoticed in production. This is a critical behavioral gap for an orchestration module.

findings.title: scheduleDaily() method is never tested
findings.description: The EveningPipeline interface exposes three public methods: recordActivity, trigger, and scheduleDaily. The scheduleDaily method (evening-pipeline.ts lines 122-127) delegates to cronService.cron() with the correct handler name and is a key part of the public contract. No test calls scheduleDaily or verifies: (1) it returns a valid schedule ID, (2) it correctly wires the 'evening-summary' handler, or (3) that a cron expression like '0 21 * * *' actually triggers the pipeline handler. While the first test uses cronService.at() for one-time scheduling, cronService.cron() for recurring schedules is a different code path that is completely untested.

findings.title: First test does not exercise createEveningPipeline at all
findings.description: The first test 'triggers evening-summary handler via CronService and produces a report' (lines 179-206) manually registers its own handler and manually calls reportGenerator.generate(). It never calls createEveningPipeline(). This test proves that CronService and ReportGenerator work individually, but it does not test the pipeline's integration wiring. Furthermore, if createEveningPipeline were called in the same test, it would register a handler with the same name 'evening-summary', creating a conflict. The test is misleadingly named as it implies it tests the evening pipeline but actually tests lower-level components in isolation. The full pipeline test (line 285) covers this path correctly, making this test partially redundant while missing the actual integration.

findings.title: trigger() error propagation not tested (no onError, throw reaches caller)
findings.description: The trigger() method (evening-pipeline.ts line 118-120) calls executeEveningPipeline() directly without a try/catch, unlike the cron handler path which wraps errors in onError. This means errors from trigger() propagate as unhandled rejections to the caller. No test verifies this behavior: that trigger() rejects with the original error (not wrapped), allowing the caller to handle it. This is a behavioral difference between cron-triggered and manual-triggered execution that should be documented and tested.

positive_observations: Real dependencies used throughout: DocumentStore with real SQLite DB in temp dirs, VectorStore with real filesystem, CronService with real SQLite, MemoryOrchestrator/Extractor/ReportGenerator with real instantiation -- no mock violations for repositories or domain objects
Test doubles for LLM and Embedding APIs are properly documented with '// Test Double rationale:' comments, correctly scoped to external services only

Full end-to-end integration test (line 285) exercises the complete pipeline path: collect activities -> report -> extract -> persist -> persona response -> vector recall, using createEveningPipeline factory with real wiring
Temp directory cleanup in afterEach prevents test pollution across runs
Empty activity boundary case is explicitly tested (line 350), verifying graceful handling of zero-activity days
Vector recall persistence is verified across logical 'day boundaries' (test at line 269), proving persist-then-recall round-trip works
